summary: System design mock interview with Tom (IGotAnOffer host/interviewer) and Dima (candidate — Google hire, competitive coder, SysDesign Meetup host). Question: design Uber. Dima covers: two-sided marketplace requirements (riders/drivers), functional and non-functional requirements, driver instant-book feature with tiered accept windows (4s vs 7s), life of a ride state machine, load balancer + per-location geo-sharding architecture, read path vs write path distinction, three logical sub-components (read path, write path/matching state machine, individual ride location beacons), high availability via Raft-style leader election, metrics and A/B testing, and interview strategy advice. Commentary sections provide tips for interview candidates.
quality_flags: Auto-transcription errors corrected where text was unintelligible (e.g., 'Riot path' → 'write path', 'buring' → 'Berlin', 'writers' → 'riders' in driver context). Commentary sections marked UNKNOWN.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, well Dima, the question I wanted to ask you today is: how would you design Uber?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: All righty, well we start from certain requirements to make sure we're solving the same problem. Usually in an interview there's a section called FRs and NFRs — functional, non-functional requirements. Personally I don't necessarily like these terms that much but they do help. So let's talk about what we need to solve for. Let's first not forget that Uber is a two-sided marketplace so we have riders — basically if I'm a rider I am at point A, or I plan to be at point A right now, sometime soon, maybe in the future, and what I do is I use the app to call a car, call a taxi, call an Uber, to get from point A to point B now or later. There also are the drivers which is a totally different part of a conversation because Uber again is a two-sided marketplace. So — let's put aside the conversation about contractors versus full-time employees — basically we're going to not touch regulation. We got a bunch of people who have signed up for the platform so that they can be given rides. I assume they pass all the background checks and they are allowed to do so and they also have the app open. They do drive people from A to B. And so to me the most interesting part of Uber is how to connect these two because solving for only riders or only drivers is not as fun as doing the whole thing, as closing the loop. But for the sake of completeness let's talk about what the riders can do. Well, book a ride from a GPS of course so that I can book the ride from where I am right now or from a given point, provided point. I can set custom time, I can change the ride midway, I can cancel it before or midway. There are tons of issues to worry about when it comes to like if it's an airport transfer, pickup certain pickup points, proper terminal locations, if there is a parking lot to enter — that's a big problem itself — I'm not sure we need to go there but I need to put it here. Pick up drop off points — also, to be honest, this would be shared requirements. And also the important thing here is that we have ratings. And when I'm the driver, I start my day, I start my shift, I'm back in business after lunch, and I open the app and I mark myself as available, which is kind of my first requirement — right, out of business on hold. And then what needs to happen is Uber as a company doesn't request rides, other riders do. So what happens when I'm marking myself as available — I'm going to call them bids — the requests to be the request from a particular rider to be taken from point A to point B. I should also add here that we have different car types — X, Black, large, and pets or whatnot, children. And I should also add that the number of people can be different. So as a driver I mark myself available in a particular car — the system knows I'm driving like a Mercedes-Benz E-class which means I can take let's say up to four people.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, as a driver, as a driver. Yeah, you need to just change that — just not to confuse the viewers. Yeah, you're absolutely right, so do not confuse the viewers — riders, drivers.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So what happens with — there's a separate conversation behind the scenes that has to do with the price — price creation. We need to talk about this eventually, I don't think it's the right time right now, but there is a price. It's in the shared requirements — price setting engine plus search pricing. Search pricing is a very clever idea: it's that if we have a hot marketplace — if it's Christmas or some large sport event — because we have so much more riders than drivers the price gets higher so that the drivers make extra money and the market works out this thing. But from what I understand, from what I know, Uber by design does not let people bid on prices. Uber sets the price itself. So it's other platforms — other platforms probably let you put the amount as the rider and potential as a driver until the orders match like in the order book — but with Uber it's a fixed price computed by the platform given the current conditions. What's important here is that when I am the driver and I receive the bid — I'm not entirely positive, it's been a while since I've seen this interface from the inside — but I don't think the driver knows a lot about the ride because I think from the product perspective there used to be a failure mode when if you want to go to a certain region where drivers don't want to go to you cannot order a car there. So I think what they would tell you as a driver is: you need to pick up somebody at a certain point — it's going to be on the map on your phone so I know how far it is. Chances are it's going to be sent to me while I'm driving a current passenger so that Uber, as a platform, really wants their drivers to be driving full-time. So when I'm completing the ride my phone will start buzzing me with potential options. And I think — I don't think it's going to tell me where the next potential rider is going — but it's going to tell me if it's a short ride or a long ride. And I also think there's a big important feature which was ultimately introduced and people loved it — drivers loved it — when they can mark themselves as going home, so that — on the one hand you don't want to penalize the riders who go to the area where drivers don't want to go — they would be embarrassing and insane — so the way it works is the platform, Uber, does not tell the driver that this potential customer wants to go to a certain region they don't want to go to. But at the same time when it's like late in my shift and I probably plan to be going home, I can tell the platform that hey I would much rather be going towards that direction. So if I'm living north of town because that's where the airport is I can probably start marking myself as please take me to rides home but not vice versa. So on heading-home mode. Yeah, so — receive pickup location, time, most often now, how long the ride is planned to be, how far, and I guess that's it — how many people, maybe, doesn't matter but not too much.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: All right. So now if this makes sense — I think it mostly does so far — let's talk about what we're actually optimizing for. Non-functional requirements — my goal, the objective, is to keep the drivers busy. I think there would also be a rating. My goal is to get the driver utilization — that's the number one priority. From what I understand about the ride-share business it's always the provider of the service, the drivers, who are in short supply. So whatever I can do algorithmically, design-wise, financially — whatever I can do to keep them more engaged, especially when it's most needed by the riders, I should be doing that. In terms of system design from the algorithm standpoint, the most interesting part I think would be to improve the algorithms of suggesting which drivers get routed to which riders because that's my core algorithm. So I'm thinking out loud here — how to approach the problem of logistics. So let's focus on the matching problem — rider-driver. Full disclosure, I have no idea how it works. I'm going to be making guesses which is probably what you guys should be doing in an interview. So let's start to postulate it from first principles. When I don't know what to say I will start saying the most obvious things and go with the flow. The most obvious thing is that — imagine I'm the rider, I'm taking my phone, I'm going to call them up, I expect someone nearby who is either idle or is about to complete their trip — I'm expecting them to be notified of my request, of my ride request. In practice I'm almost positive the way it's going to work internally is that my ride request is not sent to all the drivers in the area at the same time. I don't really want my drivers to feel like they had to compete for the ride — from the product standpoint, from the UX standpoint, if I'm the driver who wants to make money, I don't want to be distracted and frantically typing yes yes accept the ride. Once I'm about to drop off this particular passenger and I'm looking for the next one — in fact, now that I think about it, one obvious thing would be maybe a lot of drivers would actually enter this automatically-accept-rides mode. I'm not sure if Uber works this way, it might even be against certain regulations, but if I'm the assistant designer I would argue — let's extend this: also consider some automatically accept rides until let's say T plus 2 hours, 5 minutes. So obviously if I'm the driver, I have a good rating, I have a good car, everything fits — the platform, if the platform knows my default is to accept the ride — it's like with Airbnb if you have instant book. If I know that the rider has requested a ride and there is a driver available in their vicinity who's idle or about to be idle soon and who has this instant book feature on, I will just send this rider this request and assume that the request is taken.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I think UX-wise I would say I would do it like this. So we're going to be talking about the algorithm plus UX and then we'll start drawing stuff. So from the way I usually do it, I would say like — life of a ride: what happens to the ride as it progresses through the system. First of all it's requested by rider, then the system starts looking for a driver. So the main idea here is that I don't really want to be sending the same ride to multiple drivers currently simultaneously because I don't want them to be distracted. So even if let's say there are no instantly bookable drivers and there are five drivers around me, I would still not want to send a request to all of them simultaneously. Because as a driver once I'm seeing the invitation to accept the ride, I want this driver to have 5 to 10 seconds to accept this ride and they don't need to compete with each other. So like if I'm the driver, I'm dropping you off and someone else is about to take a ride from near where I am and I'm seeing the notification — hey there's a person next to me who needs a ride — as a driver I would like to design the UX and the flow so that once as a driver I see this notification I have the next, let's say, 7 seconds to decide if I accept this ride or no. For the next 7 seconds once it was sent to me, this request is potentially mine — it's not being routed to any other driver. So either I accept it within let's say 7 seconds or I don't, in which case the system finds the next potential driver. So let's say we're going to have a two-tier process: if instant book is not available, pick a driver nearby using AI for selection, send them the request, wait — I don't know — 7 seconds, go find driver. If a driver who accepts it is found, go to pickup mode. So imagine there are no instantly bookable drivers yet — I pick a driver, I send in this request, wait for 7 seconds, if no confirmation within this 7 seconds I'm going back to here in which case I'm looking for the next driver. And instant book gets priority — if an instant book driver is available nearby I would say same thing but I give them like four seconds. Up to seven if no explicit rejection. Is this idea clear? I just made this up, it could be totally off, but my point is that if you have people who are in this hot standby mode and they've committed to accept every ride, I give them like four seconds to say no. And if those guys aren't available I book a person who is not auto-confirming and I give them 7 seconds to confirm.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Now we get to the pickup mode. So to be honest in terms of system design our job is mostly done here. Pickup is pretty much Google Maps navigation or Waze or Apple Maps — I don't think we need to design this, we can use a third party app. Of course if we're a company such as Uber we have our own mapping technology and driving-specific serving capabilities. What I think we should mention here explicitly is the location is shared in real time — moreover maybe tweaked to allow for traffic lights, curbside pickup details, etc. Because I can totally imagine the first version of Uber to be using some standard Google Maps application and then I can totally imagine some standard failure modes when let's say a tiny road is one way and you probably want to be picking up the rider like 10 meters to the north because that's where it's easy to pick up this person. So it's a separate conversation, I don't think we should go there, it's a bit too low level. But fundamentally in the pickup mode, until the match has been confirmed, until we know the GPS coordinates of both phones and until we know that the driver has started the ride, we're still on the pickup mode. Pickup mode can still cancel from either side, might be for a fee. In this case if it's cancelled go back to the starting point. Once approved, once the ride is confirmed, go to in-ride. Note: confirmed includes but is not limited to GPS, manual confirmation by the driver, perhaps a code — I know there's a feature when in certain locations, in certain jurisdictions, you don't really trust people to trust each other so the platform needs to confirm that it's actually the correct rider got into the correct car. And the way Uber solves for this is they have a code like a four-digit code or QR code so the rider needs to share this with the driver and only once that is confirmed the ride can start.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: I think we've covered enough there. Shall we get to drawing?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Let's start designing. Yeah. I'm going to start from the rider which is the user because I think it's where the flow mostly — it's where the most intense flow happens. All right, system design diagrams and boxes. There are many ways to approach this. The first high level question to ask is: are we are we using any cloud provider like Amazon AWS, Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure, something else? Or are we totally bare metal like our own hardware? A good design is of course agnostic to this but it's worth mentioning. So the first box inside this cloud is a load balancer. A lot of people miss what load balancers actually do. So it's a cluster which is traditionally denoted by having a couple of them — LB cluster. The way it works is my request hits one of the instances of this load balancer. In the case of Uber it's not that important because extra 2 to 300 millisecond latency extra like 3 seconds would not make things very different — but realistically what you want to do is you want the request from the user to enter your private network as soon as possible. So load balancers are not only used to shard your backend servers so that you can add more of them — they also serve to make sure the request from the device enters my system as soon as possible and whatever network calls happen behind the scenes they happen with my own network which is controlled by me, which is low latency, high throughput, far more reliable. That's what the load balancer does here. Then it would fundamentally — there's a big thing to say here about geo-sharding. I'll get there in a moment. So fundamentally I would say hypothetically this could literally be, you know, api.uber.com — hypothetically that's the resource where the request comes to. If it's HTTP it is of course HTTPS to be safe and secure to be not interceptable at least not easily interceptable. Now behind the scenes internally I would argue, for many reasons — load balancing and regulation — different cities have their own specific sets of servers to serve the cities. So it's like a per-location Uber server. What I'm doing here is I'm starting to get some work of routing the traffic done on the load balancer level. Because once my phone talks to the load balancer, the phone knows where I am — I assume there are some GPS coordinates. So if I'm in Austin it knows I'm in Austin, if I'm in Singapore it knows I'm in Singapore. So the request of the load balancer — even even if it hits the same high level top level endpoint — there's enough metadata for the load balancer to already decide where to route it. I'm drawing multiple of these because they also shed — they know that the request would go to let's say New York, and let's say there is something else which is San Francisco — why not — let's say it's Berlin because we're international. So the load balancer at the moment it needs to make a decision where to pass this request to, it can decide which Uber location it goes to. And there could be like in Europe there could be GDPR rules — so for example per-location data for Berlin cannot leave Germany. So far the only thing I'm saying is that the moment my request, my tab, gets to the actual backend service here, it's already tied to a particular location.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Now this is a tricky part. When people draw multiple boxes like I did here it means a couple things. One thing it means is that if one of them dies the system is still up and running — that's called high availability, that's called resiliency, that's called durability. Well, durability is mostly about not losing the data but still we need multiple of these so that one of them can die. And then once we commit to having multiple nodes, it's a very delicate conversation how to proceed here because in terms of the system design architecture logically we can treat this as a single box and then decide how to best make sure it runs in a way that is highly available so nodes can die but it's still functional and durable so that once nodes die they don't lose any data. So let's pretend for a moment logically that the whole Berlin is served by a single instance of an Uber server for Berlin.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: As a high-profile engineer I would actually go as far as saying that it's not impossible because the total amount of traffic to this server — when it comes to — okay this deserves drawing — you have the so-called write path which are the mutations, state changes, and you have the read path that's immutable requests. For example when I'm just opening the app I literally want to see how many cars are there available — I don't need to go to the write path, I can go to the read path. And read path is super easy to scale because that's where strong versus eventual consistency comes into play. Read path can be eventually consistent. If I literally open the Uber app to see how many cars are there around me, if the data I'm seeing is 3, 5 seconds behind — heck even 30 seconds behind — the product still doesn't suck, it still fulfills all the requirements. So the truth is that this is super easy to scale because it can be eventually consistent. Whereas on the write path — if both of us open the app in the same location and we see slightly different pictures, that's okay. But on the write path, if both of us order a ride and we get to different servers here, and you get to server A and I get to server B, and somehow these servers are not in line with what's going on, and the same driver accepts a ride from both of us — that's a disaster, that's a catastrophe, that's what should be prevented. So logically the write path is where this single source of truth — singular, like a mutex, so to speak — synchronized section, that's where it happens. So I'm going to put it here and say: should be synchronized single source of truth. And so one of the most difficult problems of system design is how to build synchronized source of truth systems that are distributed. But logically for now we can pretend there's a single machine. And again as a high-performance engineer I know it's possible — if you take all the read path stuff, if you pretend there is enough machines that are only eventually consistent, they can be behind the world time — they might be like 5, 10, 15 seconds behind the actual state of the fleet — we ignore them for now. For the write path I'm going to claim that the total number of requests for new rides per second within the whole city of Berlin is going to be quite small. If you look at all the state transitions — pickup mode, find the driver mode, then we get the in-ride mode etc etc, then you have to confirm the ride and you may like leave a tip — if you look at all the state transitions that needs to be correctly synchronized with one another, the invariants such as no driver can accept two riders at the same time — I'm going to claim that for a single city of Berlin the number of mutating queries per second is actually quite small. It is certainly well under 1,000. I just cannot possibly imagine that on the write path in terms of synchronizing drivers and riders you order more than 1,000 rides per second. So that's actually something one machine can handle.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: In practice there will be more complexities here. Let me try to explain. In practice — so the read path is a picture of all the cars available, so to speak. The write path is the rider to driver matching state machine synchronization. We also have something in the middle which is synchronizing specific data — the data specific to a particular ride. I believe Uber has this feature — share my ride — so I can as a rider request a ride, get matched to a driver, get into the car, on a ride to the airport, and I want my wife or my peers to not be worried — so I can send them a link through the Uber app so that they can watch in real time where my location is. Of course internally even if you don't share your ride, Uber backend services absolutely do know where every driver-rider pair is. The point is — this potential component sees a lot more traffic. The state changes such as ride requested, ride cancelled, ride started, ride ended — for these I believe the traffic would be quite small for the whole city, in New York it's likely under three digits, maybe low four digits. For the data specific to individual rides, the QPS is far larger because you can imagine that let's say every five or ten seconds the driver updates their position, their coordinates, connecting to the server in real time. So the traffic here — this is the most important part — this is where we need to maintain the invariant that the same driver cannot pick up two riders. This here — the traffic is manageable by even a single machine. Here — location beacons — we have a lot more traffic. Even if it's every 5 seconds there's a beacon from every single driver currently driving for Uber or waiting to pick up someone, and for every single rider who is at least waiting for the ride or in the ride — that can easily be thousands of this driving life, so you can easily get tens of thousands of requests per second here. But the trick is for this part you cannot potentially have data from one ride colliding with data from another ride. There's no conflict, there's no room for conflicts, you can't break your logical invariants here but not here. So this basically what I'm drawing right now would be logically speaking a different sub-component of this per-location Uber server.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So this is a fleet of servers. I would argue that in practice for the read path you can add as many machines as you want — they just subscribe to the feed of internal updates. It's like a CDN more or less. To get a snapshot of which cars are currently available in Berlin, you get to one of many of these nodes and you get the data that is somewhat accurate. You just scale this as necessary — I would argue there could be dozens of them, maybe 5 to 10 for Berlin, maybe 15 for New York, maybe 50 or 100 if you don't write your code correctly. For these location beacon nodes you need a large number of them too. But each — these nodes are all identical in logic, they're all interchangeable, they're all fungible, if one of them dies you just replace it by a new one — they all serve the same purpose and have the same data. If one node dies what happens is you shard the traffic from your riders, drivers and active rides so that a particular data point in a particular time goes to a particular server. You can use consistent hashing to send it to two or three machines just to make sure if one dies they're not lost. But honestly that's not important because if a machine dies, well, what's the worst that can happen — for the next half a minute you lose track of the keep-alive messages of where the driver is. No big deal on a company scale. For the write path matching, if you're a startup that just needs to launch somehow, I would sincerely suggest that we just have a single machine for the whole Berlin that handles this. If it dies, until it restarts or until a new machine starts on a backup, for a couple seconds no new rides can be requested, no new rides can be confirmed or cancelled or whatnot. But in practice if you are the big Uber itself you don't do it on a single machine — that's too dangerous. You have a few of them. The standard technique is to use data replications — have an odd number of machines, traditionally would be three or five, so that they always are in touch with each other, they always know which one is the main one, who's whose word matters most in a democratic system, so that if one of these machines dies — if it's not the leader that's okay, it's replaced and kept up to date. If the leader dies, within a split second another leader is elected and they guarantee that all the invariants we need to respect are being respected. That's my high level diagram.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, that's interesting. I wanted to ask you: what metrics are you going to be tracking here? Like, how are you going to know if the system is behaving as it should do?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Oh, that's a big question — there are tons of directions, tons of ways to postulate the question actually. So like, bottom up, as an engineer I want to make sure that my system is up and running. So I would look at the traffic numbers everywhere — traffic numbers here, traffic numbers to every single location, traffic numbers from within internal systems to all these components, all these three. If any of them drop something is wrong — that's something I can totally monitor myself. As a higher level system designer I would also argue we need to track user-facing metrics. I want to know how many rides are currently taking place. I would imagine — imagine Berlin has an Uber office, I don't think it's the case but it doesn't matter — I would imagine there's going to be a large screen to monitor real-time metrics with tons of charts. It will be like active real-time number of rides happening right now. There will be like this daily sine wave because there is more at night than in the afternoon and if suddenly it goes below what you would expect or above what you would expect this should trigger an alarm, an alert, somebody should look into this. I would look into the length of the rides if the traffic pattern has changed. I would look into the ratings of drivers and riders — we clearly want to not deteriorate in performance for the end users. I would absolutely look at more fine-grained metrics such as like average distance from the rider to a driver, average pickup time. Because in practice there's going to be tons of A/B testing behind the scenes. If my engineering team has designed a new matching algorithm that is presumably making pickups faster, chances are it's being A/B tested for parts of drivers and I want to see how it performs, how it behaves so they can make decisions on how to roll it out and when. So yeah tons of cool stuff.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Thank you for a good question — thanks for the answer. Also, just before we wind this up, are there any other kind of features that you think might be interesting to add and how would they affect your design?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, let me reflect. Let me look back at this as I slowly exit the mode of someone who was interviewed and I enter the mode of the hiring committee member. I think I've tried to give your listeners the best possible experience of seeing how the real interview looks like in practice. If I'm being interviewed like this I would absolutely ask what are we designing for real because nobody designs Ubers these days from scratch. Either it's a smaller startup so that it's not a replica of Uber but rather some more targeted niche product — I don't know, maybe it's a rideshare pickup, maybe they are allowed to use pedestrian roads, maybe they have drones, maybe they have extra regulations on what business advantage they have compared to Uber itself — in which case the design should absolutely be focused on this particular tailored mode. On the other hand if you really need to design Uber, well, I can guarantee you as someone who's in the industry for a long time, this is not how Uber looks like on a high level. Yes. But as we all know from Twitter and Elon Musk, high level whiteboard designs have little to do with what systems look like behind the scenes. So for the interview conversation I think that's approximately what I would imagine candidates should draw when asked this question. But if I'm interviewing — given I'm a couple levels above the senior staff level — I will probably be having a more product-first conversation. I think I managed to strike the balance and actually talk about consistency, about data flows, about databases somehow. But yeah, it's a really big can of worms. And if I can leave you with one thing here — you got to read your interview. You got to figure out what this particular company is looking for. Uber in particular is well known for looking for product-first engineers. If you actually talk to your interviewer about hey I need to optimize like curbside pickup, I need to suggest pickup spots because that's where riders are more happy to be picked up compared to other pickup spots where they complain like they were honked at — this is super useful, this gives you tons of bonus points for Uber. For other companies do your homework. But yeah tons of ways to go from here.

training_pairs.question: How would you design Uber?
training_pairs.answer: All righty, well we start from certain requirements to make sure we're solving the same problem. Usually in an interview there's a section called FRs and NFRs — functional, non-functional requirements. Personally I don't necessarily like these terms that much but they do help. So let's talk about what we need to solve for. Let's first not forget that Uber is a two-sided marketplace so we have riders — basically if I'm a rider I am at point A, and I use the app to call a car, call a taxi, call an Uber, to get from point A to point B now or later. There also are the drivers which is a totally different part of a conversation because Uber again is a two-sided marketplace. The most interesting part of Uber is how to connect these two because solving for only riders or only drivers is not as fun as doing the whole thing, as closing the loop.
training_pairs.follow_up: What are the functional requirements for riders and drivers?

training_pairs.question: What are the functional requirements for riders? And what happens on the driver side?
training_pairs.answer: For riders: book a ride from GPS, so I can book the ride from where I am right now or from a given point. I can set custom time, I can change the ride midway, I can cancel it before or midway. There are pickup-specific issues — airport transfers, terminal locations, parking lot entry — I'll note them here. Ratings are also a shared requirement. On the driver side: when I'm the driver, I start my shift and I open the app and I mark myself as available. Uber as a company doesn't request rides — other riders do. When I'm marking myself available I start receiving bids — the requests from a particular rider to be taken from point A to B. We have different car types — X, Black, large, and pets or whatnot, children. Number of people can be different. The price is a shared requirement too — Uber by design does not let people bid on prices. Uber sets the price itself given current conditions, including surge pricing during high demand events. When I receive a bid as a driver, I'm told to pick up somebody at a certain point — it's on the map on my phone. I'll know how far it is. It'll likely be sent to me while I'm completing a current ride. There's also an important feature — drivers can mark themselves as going home, so the platform can bias toward giving them rides in that direction without penalizing riders going to areas drivers don't want to go to.
training_pairs.follow_up: What are the non-functional requirements — what are we optimizing for?

training_pairs.question: What are the non-functional requirements? What are we optimizing for?
training_pairs.answer: My goal, the objective, is to keep the drivers busy. From what I understand about the ride-share business it's always the provider of the service, the drivers, who are in short supply. So whatever I can do algorithmically, design-wise, financially — whatever I can do to keep them more engaged, especially when it's most needed by the riders, I should be doing that. In terms of system design from the algorithm standpoint, the most interesting part I think would be to improve the algorithms of suggesting which drivers get routed to which riders. That's my core algorithm.
training_pairs.follow_up: Walk me through the life of a ride request.

training_pairs.question: Walk me through the life of a ride request from request to completion.
training_pairs.answer: Life of a ride: it's requested by rider, then the system starts looking for a driver. The main idea here is that I don't really want to be sending the same ride to multiple drivers simultaneously because I don't want them to be distracted. So even if there are no instantly bookable drivers and there are five drivers around me, I would still not want to send a request to all of them simultaneously. I want this driver to have 5 to 10 seconds to accept this ride and they don't need to compete with each other. So once as a driver I see this notification I have the next let's say 7 seconds to decide if I accept this ride or no. For the next 7 seconds once it was sent to me, this request is potentially mine — it's not being routed to any other driver. So either I accept it within 7 seconds or I don't, in which case the system finds the next potential driver. Two-tier process: if instant book is not available, pick a driver nearby using AI for selection, send them the request, wait 7 seconds, go find next driver. Instant book gets priority — if an instant book driver is available nearby, I give them like 4 seconds to reject. If no explicit rejection, the ride is taken. In pickup mode: both phones' GPS coordinates are known and the driver heads to the rider. Either side can still cancel, might be for a fee. If cancelled, go back to the starting point. Once confirmed — via GPS, manual confirmation, or a four-digit code or QR code in some jurisdictions — the ride starts and we move to in-ride mode.
training_pairs.follow_up: Walk me through the high-level architecture.

training_pairs.question: Walk me through the high-level architecture.
training_pairs.answer: The first box inside the cloud is a load balancer. A lot of people miss what load balancers actually do. It's a cluster. The way it works is my request hits one of the instances of this load balancer. Load balancers are not only used to shard your backend servers so that you can add more of them — they also serve to make sure the request from the device enters my system as soon as possible and whatever network calls happen behind the scenes they happen with my own network which is controlled by me, which is low latency, high throughput, far more reliable. That's what the load balancer does. Then — there's a big thing to say here about geo-sharding. Different cities have their own specific sets of servers to serve the cities. So it's like a per-location Uber server. The load balancer, knowing the rider's GPS from their request metadata, can route to the right location's servers. This also satisfies data residency requirements — for example GDPR may require that European user data, like Berlin's data, not leave Germany.
training_pairs.follow_up: What's the key architectural distinction between the read path and write path?

training_pairs.question: What's the key architectural distinction between the read path and write path, and why does it matter?
training_pairs.answer: You have the write path which are the mutations, state changes, and you have the read path that's immutable requests. Read path can be eventually consistent. If I literally open the Uber app to see how many cars are there around me, if the data I'm seeing is 3, 5 seconds behind — heck even 30 seconds behind — the product still doesn't suck. So the read path is super easy to scale. Whereas on the write path — if both of us order a ride and we get to different servers, and somehow these servers are not in line with what's going on, and the same driver accepts a ride from both of us — that's a disaster, that's a catastrophe, that's what should be prevented. So logically the write path is where this single source of truth — synchronized section — that's where it happens. For a single city like Berlin the number of mutating queries per second is actually quite small. It is certainly well under 1,000. That's actually something one machine can handle.
training_pairs.follow_up: Can you break down the three logical sub-components?

training_pairs.question: Can you walk through the three logical sub-components and how you'd scale each?
training_pairs.answer: I'd break it into three: the read path, the write path matching state machine, and individual ride location beacons. For the read path — it's a picture of all available cars. You can add as many machines as you want — they just subscribe to the feed of internal updates, like a CDN. 5 to 10 for Berlin, maybe 15 for New York. For the write path matching — the invariant is no driver can accept two riders at the same time. For a startup, just use a single machine for the whole city. For big Uber, you have an odd number of machines — traditionally three or five — using Raft-style leader election. If one machine dies — if it's not the leader, that's okay, it's replaced and kept up to date. If the leader dies, within a split second another leader is elected and the invariants are always maintained. For individual ride location beacons — every 5 to 10 seconds each driver's phone sends a position update. You can easily get tens of thousands of requests per second here. But there are no conflicts between them — shard by driver ID using consistent hashing, keep two to three replicas per shard. If a node dies you lose a few seconds of location data at most, which is acceptable.
training_pairs.follow_up: What metrics are you going to be tracking? How will you know if the system is behaving correctly?

training_pairs.question: What metrics are you going to be tracking? How will you know if the system is behaving as it should?
training_pairs.answer: Oh, that's a big question — there are tons of directions. Bottom up, as an engineer I want to make sure that my system is up and running. So I would look at traffic numbers everywhere — traffic numbers to every single location, traffic numbers from within internal systems to all the components. If any of them drop something is wrong. As a higher level system designer I would also argue we need to track user-facing metrics. I want to know how many rides are currently taking place. I would imagine there's going to be a large screen to monitor real-time metrics with tons of charts. It will be like active real-time number of rides happening right now — there will be like this daily sine wave because there is more at night than in the afternoon and if suddenly it goes below what you would expect or above what you would expect this should trigger an alarm, an alert, somebody should look into this. I would look into the length of the rides if the traffic pattern has changed. I would look into the ratings of drivers and riders. I would absolutely look at more fine-grained metrics such as average distance from the rider to a driver, average pickup time. Because in practice there's going to be tons of A/B testing behind the scenes. If my engineering team has designed a new matching algorithm that is presumably making pickups faster, chances are it's being A/B tested for parts of drivers and I want to see how it performs, how it behaves so they can make decisions on how to roll it out and when.
training_pairs.follow_up: Are there any other features that might be interesting to add and how would they affect your design?

training_pairs.question: Are there any other interesting features to add and how would they affect your design?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, let me reflect. If I'm being interviewed like this I would absolutely ask what are we designing for real because nobody designs Ubers these days from scratch. Either it's a smaller startup with a more targeted niche product — maybe a rideshare pickup with drones, maybe extra regulations, what business advantage they have compared to Uber itself — in which case the design should absolutely be focused on this particular tailored mode. On the other hand if you really need to design Uber, I can guarantee you as someone who's in the industry for a long time, this is not how Uber looks like on a high level. High level whiteboard designs have little to do with what systems look like behind the scenes. But for the interview conversation I think that's approximately what candidates should draw when asked this question. But if I'm interviewing — given I'm a couple levels above the senior staff level — I will probably be having a more product-first conversation. And if I can leave you with one thing here — you got to read your interview. You got to figure out what this particular company is looking for. Uber in particular is well known for looking for product-first engineers. If you talk about curbside pickup optimization, suggesting pickup spots because that's where riders are happier and don't get honked at — this gives you tons of bonus points for Uber. For other companies do your homework.
